Redox reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms are essentially the underpinning basis for exercise metabolism during endurance-type exercise, because it is the transfer of hydrogen atoms from the food we eat to oxygen molecules (to produce water) which produces the energy store (ATP) to fuel our muscles during exercise. In such circumstances, oxygen is said to be the final *electron acceptor*, as it undergoes a four-electron reduction (by accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water. Collectively, this process is known as *oxidative phosphorylation* and we will cover this in detail in Chapter 5.

Oxidation reactions are usually exergonic reactions as they involve the loss of hydrogen atoms from energy-rich reduced compounds (containing many hydrogen atoms) to lower energy compounds. In the case of oxidation of food such as carbohydrates, some of the energy released through the breakage of bonds (i.e. removal of hydrogen atoms) is used to phosphorylate adenosine diphosphate (ADP) to ATP (i.e. *substrate phosphorylation*). The energy has therefore been transferred and stored in ATP molecules so that it can later be used to drive energy-consuming processes such as exercise.

## Definition

Redox reactions in exercise metabolism are reactions involving the transfer of hydrogen atoms (and their associated electrons) from energy-rich reduced food substrates (notably carbohydrates such as glucose) to oxygen, producing water and releasing energy that is captured as ATP to fuel muscular work.

## Core Principles

- Hydrogen atom transfer from food substrates to oxygen is the underpinning basis of endurance exercise metabolism.
- Oxygen serves as the final *electron acceptor*, undergoing a four-electron reduction (accepting four hydrogen atoms) to produce water.
- This coupled process of hydrogen/electron transfer to oxygen with concomitant ATP synthesis is collectively known as *oxidative phosphorylation*.
- Oxidation reactions are typically exergonic: hydrogen atoms are lost from energy-rich reduced compounds (containing many hydrogen atoms) and transferred to lower-energy compounds, releasing free energy.
- A portion of the energy released by bond breakage (removal of hydrogen atoms) during oxidation of carbohydrates is used to phosphorylate ADP to ATP via *substrate phosphorylation*.
- Energy is thereby transferred and stored in ATP molecules, available to drive energy-consuming processes such as exercise.
- Electron transfer and oxidative phosphorylation are conceptually distinct from, but coupled to, upstream catabolic pathways (glycolysis, the link reaction, and the TCA cycle), which generate the reduced hydrogen carriers that feed electrons into the oxidative phosphorylation machinery.
- Carbohydrate metabolism for redox-driven ATP production is organised through interlinked pathways — glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, and glycogenesis — that govern substrate availability for oxidation.
- Carbohydrate fuels for oxidation are built from monosaccharide units (glucose and its isomers, fructose), which combine via glycoside bonds to form disaccharides, and further into polysaccharide variants; in muscle and liver, these are stored as structurally organised glycogen granules that serve as the substrate reservoir for glycogenolysis.
- The general structure of glucose and its isomers, and of fructose (including its formation), can be represented at the molecular level, with the glycoside bond formed at a defined position to link monosaccharide units into disaccharides.
- Lactic acid formation represents an alternative fate of pyruvate when oxidative capacity is exceeded, with subsequent fates including oxidation back to pyruvate, gluconeogenic recycling, or use as a fuel by other tissues.
- The link reaction and the TCA cycle comprise a defined set of key reactions that connect glycolytic end-products to the reduced hydrogen carriers used in oxidative phosphorylation.
- Complete aerobic oxidation of a glucose molecule yields substantially more ATP than anaerobic breakdown, reflecting the additional ATP captured through electron transfer to oxygen relative to substrate phosphorylation alone; the net ATP yield can be calculated explicitly for both anaerobic and aerobic breakdown of glucose.
- ATP turnover during high-intensity exercise is supported by three integrated systems — PCr hydrolysis, glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation — whose relative contributions shift across repeated bouts: as average power output declines between sprints, the ATP turnover rate falls, the glycolytic contribution is significantly reduced, and oxidative phosphorylation (CHO oxidation) can become the major contributor to ATP production after only six seconds of sprinting in a later (e.g., third) bout.
- Quantitative partitioning of ATP provision across PCr hydrolysis, glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation between sprint 1 and sprint 3 (e.g., as depicted in Figure 11.10) demonstrates that the absolute ATP turnover rate decreases in parallel with declining average power output, while the relative reliance on oxidative phosphorylation rises.
- High rates of glycogen breakdown during initial sprint exercise depend on both transformational and post-transformational regulation of phosphorylase activity. Transformational regulation is mediated by Ca²⁺ release acting on phosphorylase kinase (converting phosphorylase *b* to *a*), while post-transformational regulation is achieved by increased substrate provision of Pᵢ (supplied from *PCr hydrolysis*) and positive allosteric modulators such as AMP.
- Reduced rates of glycogenolysis in the latter half of an initial sprint and throughout subsequent sprints coincide with reversion of phosphorylase *a* to *b*, suggesting that Ca²⁺-mediated phosphorylase transformation is overridden by other factors, potentially H⁺-induced inhibition of phosphorylase kinase.

## Sub-concepts

- *Electron acceptor* (oxygen as the terminal acceptor in aerobic metabolism)
- *Oxidative phosphorylation* (covered in detail in Chapter 5)
- *Substrate phosphorylation* (direct ADP → ATP phosphorylation coupled to substrate oxidation)
- Exergonic oxidation of carbohydrates as a fuel source
- Role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise
- Structural basis of carbohydrate fuels: glucose and its isomers, fructose (including its formation), disaccharides formed via glycoside bonds, polysaccharide variations, and glycogen granule architecture
- Glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, and glycogenesis as the carbohydrate pathways feeding (or replenishing) substrates for oxidation
- Glycolysis, the link reaction, and the TCA cycle as the upstream pathways generating reduced hydrogen carriers for electron transfer
- Key reactions of the link reaction and the TCA cycle
- Electron transfer as a concept linking reduced hydrogen carriers to the terminal electron acceptor
- Anaerobic vs aerobic ATP yield from glucose breakdown (quantitative ATP accounting)
- Formation and fates of lactic acid as an outcome of anaerobic glucose metabolism
- *PCr hydrolysis* as a rapid ATP-buffering system and source of Pᵢ for glycogenolysis
- Integrated contributions of PCr hydrolysis, glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation to ATP turnover during repeated sprints
- Quantitative shift in ATP turnover and pathway contributions between sprint 1 and sprint 3 (declining ATP turnover rate; reduced glycolytic contribution; oxidative phosphorylation as major contributor after ~6 s in bout 3)
- Transformational vs post-transformational regulation of glycogen phosphorylase (Ca²⁺/phosphorylase kinase; Pᵢ and AMP as allosteric activators; H⁺-induced inhibition)
- Reversion of phosphorylase *a* to *b* as a mechanism limiting glycogenolysis in later sprint phases and subsequent bouts

## Practical Applications

- Endurance exercise relies on aerobic redox reactions to sustain ATP supply for working muscles.
- Understanding the link between hydrogen transfer, oxygen consumption, and ATP yield underpins the rationale for measuring oxygen uptake (e.g., VO₂) as an index of energy metabolism during exercise.
- Quantifying ATP produced from a glucose molecule under anaerobic versus aerobic conditions provides a basis for understanding the metabolic cost and fuel efficiency of different exercise intensities.
- Appreciating the role of carbohydrates (glucose, fructose, disaccharides, glycogen) in sport and exercise underlies nutritional strategies for fuelling training and competition.
- Knowledge of carbohydrate structure — from monosaccharides (glucose isomers, fructose) through glycoside-bonded disaccharides and polysaccharide variants to glycogen granules — informs choices of carbohydrate type and form for pre-, during-, and post-exercise feeding.
- Recognising the formation and fates of lactic acid (oxidation, gluconeogenic recycling, inter-tissue shuttling) shapes interpretation of blood lactate measurements and recovery strategies.
- During repeated-sprint exercise, the progressive shift of ATP provision toward oxidative phosphorylation (with diminished glycolytic contribution) informs training prescription and recovery strategies aimed at sustaining power output across bouts.
- The observation that oxidative phosphorylation can become the dominant ATP source after only ~6 s of sprinting by the third bout highlights the importance of aerobic capacity for repeated-sprint performance, even in events traditionally considered anaerobic.
- Recognizing that accumulating H⁺ may inhibit phosphorylase kinase and reduce glycogenolytic flux helps explain fatigue patterns in repeated high-intensity efforts and supports interventions targeting acid–base balance.
